purpose: Pinned pipelines for WS5 emitted-proof-size measurements on the R(3,k) upper-half family. The measured quantity is S(F; encoding, solver, config, seed) — the size of the proof THIS pipeline emits. It is an achievable upper bound on proof length under this pipeline, NOT a minimal certificate; no claim about shortest proofs is made (charter section 4, WS5). Two pipelines are registered; they differ ONLY in the encoding's clause emission order — the SAME clause set and variable numbering under two orders (the encoding-variance axis the charter requires before any family trend is read). Solver, config, seeds, and checker are shared.

encodings.name: r3k-edge-cnf
encodings.generator_invocation: python3 tools/gen_r3k_cnf.py <k> <n> -o problem.cnf   (--order lex is the default)
encodings.clause_order: lex: ALL red-triangle clauses (lexicographic 3-subset order), then ALL blue-K_k clauses (lexicographic k-subset order)
encodings.description: Variables = edges of K_n in lexicographic order (true = red). Clauses: one all-negative clause per 3-subset (no red triangle), one all-positive clause per k-subset (no blue K_k); red block then blue block, each in lexicographic subset order. Deterministic and byte-frozen: the default output's sha256s are regression-tested in tests/test_gen_r3k_cnf.py. Logically identical to certificates/ramsey-3-3/problem.cnf (same clause set, different clause order).

encodings.name: r3k-edge-cnf
encodings.clause_order: interleaved: the same two lexicographic clause streams alternate (red_1, blue_1, red_2, blue_2, ...); when the shorter stream is exhausted, the remainder of the longer one follows. At k=3 both streams run over the same 3-subsets, so this is the negative/positive pair-per-triangle order of the committed exemplar.
encodings.relation_to_v1_0_0: SAME clause set and variable numbering as v1.0.0 — ONLY the clause emission order and the header comments differ (unit-tested: tests/test_gen_r3k_cnf.py, test_interleaved_same_clause_set_different_order). At (k=3, n=6) the output is byte-identical to certificates/ramsey-3-3/problem.cnf, the exemplar produced by an independent earlier session (regression-tested: test_interleaved_reproduces_committed_file_byte_identically).
encodings.description: Same variables and clause set as v1.0.0; emission order interleaves the red and blue streams pair-wise. Header = the exemplar's single-line format (at k=3 the exemplar's exact phrasing, which is what gives byte-identity with the committed file; at k>3 the honest asymmetric phrasing in the same format). The emitted file carries no encoding-version stamp in its comments; its identity is pinned by this manifest and by the generation command recorded with every measurement.

pipelines.note: the original pipeline; every measurement recorded before the encoding-variance axis was added runs under this id

identical to r3k-cadical300-drat-v1 in solver, config, seeds, checker, and caps; differs ONLY in the encoding's clause order — the second point on the encoding-variance axis. Per the pinning discipline, its measurements form their own series and are never mixed into the v1 curve.

solver.name: cadical
solver.config: default options; plain-text DRAT forced with --no-binary; seed set with --seed
solver.seed_mechanism: cadical's built-in --seed=<n> option (present in this version, so no clause-shuffling workaround is used). Observed under both encoding orders: at R(3,3) n=6 the run is seed-invariant (byte-identical proofs across seeds 1-3); at R(3,4) n=9 seeds produce distinct proofs.

checker.name: drat-trim
checker.build: cc -O2 -o drat-trim drat-trim.c (from a clean checkout of the pinned commit)
checker.verdict_rule: PASS iff stdout contains the substring 's VERIFIED'. The exit code is never trusted (drat-trim can exit 0 on both outcomes), and the substring is not anchored at line start (progress output uses carriage returns).
checker.negative_control: a deliberately truncated proof must yield 's NOT VERIFIED'

byte_size_definition: emitted_drat_bytes = the size in bytes (st_size) of the plain-text DRAT file exactly as written by the solver invocation above. No trimming (drat-trim's optimized core is NOT the measured object), no compression, no post-processing.
timing_definition: solve_seconds_wall and check_seconds_wall are wall-clock seconds for the solver/checker process on one arm64 macOS machine (Darwin 24.5.0); indicative only, not a pinned quantity.

caps_for_attempts.note: an aborted run is recorded as DNF — a result, not a gap
